exemplar.title: To what extent can we see the change in gentrification in the Bermuda Triangle area further from the city center?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can we see the change in gentrification in the Bermuda Triangle area further from the city center?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, focused geographical research question with testable hypotheses linked to urban-geography theory
Precise identification and mapping of a well-defined study area enabling feasible primary data collection
High-quality locational maps at multiple scales employing conventions for clear spatial representation
weaknesses: Incomplete linkage of some hypotheses to specific theory in the syllabus reference
Inconsistent use of mapping conventions (missing north arrows/scale bars on some maps)
Some figure captions lack sequential numbering and detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands met the descriptors for band 3: the question is fully geographical with clear theoretical links; the context is specific and feasible; mapping conventions are largely respected. Minor lapses in theory linkage and map conventions were noted but do not detract from the overall excellence of this section.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive explanation of multiple methods explicitly linked to each hypothesis and urban-geography theory
Correct implementation of primary techniques (building surveys, QDI) for reliable data collection
weaknesses: Modest sample size in some zones and reliance on one secondary dataset reduces overall data quality
Method tables and formulae occasionally lack clarity (missing legends, undefined variables)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are well described and theory-aligned (band 3), but uneven data reliability and some implementation details limit this to band 2 overall. The section demonstrates clear understanding, though high-quality data collection is not uniformly achieved.

strengths: All collected datasets are relevant to the hypotheses, providing sufficient information for basic analysis
Use of varied presentation techniques (choropleth, scatter plots with trend lines, composite charts) appropriate to data types
Most figures follow conventions with legends and labels
weaknesses: Some data gaps (e.g., incomplete building-classification table) limit analytical depth
Frequent minor errors in presentation (spelling mistakes, inconsistent numbering, missing units)
Conventions are followed overall but occasional lapses occur
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and presentation are adequate for meaningful analysis (band 3), but neither are fully exemplary. Presentation techniques are appropriate, yet minor convention errors and data sufficiency gaps prevent a band 4â6 score.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Application of appropriate descriptive statistics and Pearson correlation to test hypotheses
Identification and geographical interpretation of trends and anomalies linked to theory
Coverage of all three hypotheses with coherent discussion of results
weaknesses: Significance testing and confidence intervals are omitted, leaving statistical interpretation incomplete
Zones 8â9 are excluded from some graphs without sufficient justification
Analysis has moderate evidential gaps and lacks deeper exploration of anomalies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis employs valid techniques and links patterns to theory (band 4), but incomplete statistical rigor and unexplained data omissions limit this to band 3 overall. The section is solid but not comprehensive.

strengths: Clear conclusion that revisits each hypothesis with data support
Use of specific findings (e.g., correlation values) to justify conclusions
weaknesses: Conclusion does not explicitly address the extent to which the fieldwork question was answered
Text is truncated in places, reducing coherence and completeness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clearly supported by analysis and summarizes key findings (band 2). Minor structural and completeness issues do not warrant deduction beyond the maximum for this criterion.

strengths: Acknowledges methodological strengths (e.g., systematic sampling, mixed methods)
weaknesses: Improvement suggestions are superficial, lacking explanation of how changes would impact results
Depth of evaluation is limited with only general listing of strengths/weaknesses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines relevant strengths and suggestions but remains at a superficial level (band 1). Lacking detailed improvements or impact discussion prevents higher marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Four scale maps (Poland, WrocÅaw, district, study area) include legends, scales and north arrows, enabling the reader to locate every zone and site.  Mapping conventions are largely respected, so spatial representation is clear and effective.

The student formulates a clear, concise and fully geographical question that explicitly investigates gentrification patterns with distance from the city centre.  The concept of gentrification is drawn from urban-geography theory and is developed into three testable hypotheses, demonstrating sound theoretical linkage and providing a solid basis for subsequent investigation.

A varied suite of presentation techniques is used (choropleth QDI map, scatter graphs with trend lines/RÂ², bar-line composite chart).  These are appropriate and mostly effective, exceeding basic expectations even if not all figures are flawless.

Primary techniques (building surveys, QDI) are correctly implemented, but the sample size per zone is modest and one dataset (property prices) is secondary.  Consequently, data reliability is acceptable yet not consistently âhigh-qualityâ across all variables.

The student applies descriptive statistics and a Pearson correlation coefficient, correctly interpreting an r value.  While confidence limits are absent, the analytical tools selected are appropriate and executed without major error.

Trends and anomalies are identified for each hypothesis and linked back to retail theory and gentrification concepts.  The narrative goes beyond listing results, providing meaningful geographical interpretation, though not wholly exhaustive.

A precise inner-city district (PrzedmieÅcie OÅawskie â âBermuda Triangleâ) is identified, and its boundaries are shown.  Historical and socioeconomic context is outlined, explaining why the area is suitable for primary data collection on gentrification.  The specificity of nine zones and mapped sites confirms clear feasibility for fieldwork.

The evaluation outlines several relevant strengths (systematic sampling, mixed methods) and weaknesses (excluded variables), showing an adequate but not detailed appreciation of methodological quality.

The three datasets collected are generally relevant to the hypotheses and provide enough information to attempt statistical and descriptive analysis, though gaps appear (e.g., incomplete building-classification table).  Overall sufficiency is moderate rather than exemplary.

The conclusion revisits each hypothesis and summarises key findings, citing specific data (e.g., r = 0.82).  Despite brief discussion of anomalies, support remains mainly descriptive, but it is clearly grounded in the preceding analysis.

Analysis covers all three hypotheses but omits significance testing and excludes Zones 8-9 from some graphs without full justification, leaving moderate evidential gaps.  Coverage is therefore solid yet not complete.

Methods for each hypothesis are explained in detail (systematic sampling grid, building-function tally, QDI survey, use of online price database).  Each method is explicitly linked to the relevant hypothesis and urban-geography theory, demonstrating comprehensive alignment.

Most figures contain legends, axis labels and scales, but minor errors persist (spelling, inconsistent figure numbering, missing units in one chart).  Conventions are followed on the whole with only occasional lapses.

Improvement suggestions are present but remain general (e.g., consider transport access) with no explanation of how changes would affect results.  The discussion therefore stays at a superficial level.

annotations.comment: The cityâs historical context is outlined, but adding recent socio-economic or demographic statistics would deepen the geographic framing and relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The self-made study area map clearly delineates zones, but it would benefit from consistent use of mapping conventions such as legend placement and orientation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The syllabus reference lists topics but does not explicitly connect them to this investigationâlink specific theory to hypotheses for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical and grounded in urban theory, facilitating testable hypotheses and a focused investigation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The student effectively links the working-class history of the Bermuda Triangle to early signs of gentrification, showing good grasp of geographic context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The detailed district map is useful but lacks a north arrow and scale barâadding these would improve spatial conventions and readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The justification for Hypothesis 1 mentions demand patterns but should explicitly reference central place theory (e.g., Berry) to strengthen theoretical linkage.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The third hypothesis justification is cut off mid-sentence, indicating incomplete reasoning; ensure full elaboration for each hypothesis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The building function categorization table is comprehensive, but the use of color coding lacks an example legendâadd a sample key for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The systematic sampling grid is well described, but discuss potential sampling bias or site accessibility issues to evaluate reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The formula for the mean is included, but the variables (sum of terms, n) are not definedâclarify each symbol for reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The QDI descriptor table provides detail but lacks a concise heading; grouping descriptors by score bands would improve readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The presentation of low-order store percentages is clear and directly supports Hypothesis 1 with precise zone data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 10 effectively illustrates the trend, but axis labels lack units and the trendline equation is omittedâadd both for full clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The weak correlation (r = 0.002) is reported but lacks interpretation of what near-zero implies for hypothesis 3; discuss possible reasons and data limitations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The conclusion summarizes findings but does not explicitly return to the core fieldwork questionâs scope; add a clear statement on âto what extent.â
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: The last paragraph in the conclusions is truncated (âwhile inâ); ensure all sentences are complete to convey a cohesive argument.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: The evaluation acknowledges appropriate site choice effectively, demonstrating depth in discussing methodological strengths.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: External factors are noted but the student does not outline how transport access or policy initiatives could quantitatively alter results; elaborate improvements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: The country-scale map effectively locates WrocÅaw but omits scale and legend; add these to meet mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure captions for multiple self-made maps lack sequential numbering and detailâensure consistent labeling (Figure 2, Figure 3, etc.) and descriptive text.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The Pearson correlation formula is correctly displayed, but the student does not specify a significance threshold (e.g., p-value) or degrees of freedom.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The report states r = 0.82 but does not discuss confidence intervals or test significance; include this to strengthen statistical validity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The caption misspells âAverageâ as âAvrageâ and lacks description of axesâcorrect spelling and add axis definitions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The outliers in the building quality analysis are noted, but the student could explore socio-economic or policy factors explaining these anomalies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis
